observations: Ground-mounted commercial or utility-scale solar array visible across an open paddock — multiple rows of panels on steel racking, good southerly-facing orientation.
No workers, customers, or installation activity visible in frame — this appears to be a reference site or demonstration asset being shown to a prospective customer.
Partly cloudy sky — useful context for a customer conversation about generation variability and system sizing.

salesPitchFeedback.message: CRITICAL pitch problem: 'Everyone on this street is getting it' is a pressure tactic that erodes trust instantly. Australian consumers are savvy — this line often triggers scepticism and objections rather than urgency. Remove it entirely.

CRITICAL: 'Don't worry about the details, just sign here today' is the fastest way to lose a sale AND attract an ACCC complaint. Customers have a right to understand what they're signing. This line risks a cooling-off cancellation and a reputation hit.

No payback period or savings figure was mentioned. Customers make decisions on return on investment — always anchor the conversation with a specific number, e.g. 'A system this size typically pays itself off in 4 to 6 years and saves around $2,000 a year on power bills.'

Warranty and after-sales support were not raised. These are top objections for Australian solar buyers — address them proactively: 'Your panels carry a 25-year performance warranty and the inverter is covered for 10 years. We handle all warranty claims directly.'
No rapport-building detected before moving to the close. Start with a question about their current power bill or usage habits to personalise the pitch before presenting a solution.

STRONGER LINE to replace the pressure tactic — try: 'A lot of homes in this area have already cut their bills significantly with solar — I can show you exactly what the numbers would look like for your place, no obligation.'

STRONGER CLOSE to replace 'just sign here' — try: 'I want to make sure this is the right fit for you. Can I walk you through the proposal so you feel confident before we go ahead? You've got a 10-day cooling-off period as well, so there's no pressure.'

Use this site as a visual proof point — point at the array and say: 'This is similar to what we install. Systems like this have been running for years with zero maintenance issues. That's the quality we bring to residential installs.'

timeOnTaskNote: Pitch phase — no installation activity visible. If this site visit is purely for demonstration, keep the customer walk-around to under 10 minutes and transition to the proposal conversation promptly to respect their time.
nextSteps: Immediately drop the pressure-tactic language and rebuild trust by asking the customer an open question about their power bills or energy goals.
Walk the customer through a written proposal or tablet-based quote with real savings numbers and payback period before asking for any signature.
Highlight the warranty terms and your company's local after-sales support to address likely objections before they are raised.

visualCallouts.label: Upsell opportunity
visualCallouts.message: Point at this array and say: this scale shows what solar can do — even a small residential system delivers the same technology and the same savings per kilowatt.

visualCallouts.label: Address cloudy sky
visualCallouts.message: Customer may worry about cloudy days — say: even on overcast days like this the panels still generate around 20 to 30 percent output, and your feed-in tariff and battery option cover the gaps.

spokenCue.say: Drop the pressure line — ask about their power bill first.
